expected: Per the API docs, a successful cancel request returns the order with status:'cancelled' and a non-null cancelled_at timestamp.
observed: The request returned 200 (success), but the response body still shows status:'confirmed' and cancelled_at:null.
ground_truth: Cancellation is processed asynchronously via a queue; the response is built from a read of the order taken before the write completes, so 200 is returned before the state actually changes. Polling GET /orders/482910 a few seconds later shows status:'cancelled'. It is an eventual-consistency lag, not a failed cancellation.

context.note: Same cart contents and same promo code, submitted 3 minutes earlier by the same session.

expected: Promo code SAVE10 is documented as a fixed 10% discount; repeated use in the same session should apply identically.
observed: This call applied 15% instead of 10% for the identical cart and code, and the response references a different promo_rule_id than the earlier call.
ground_truth: A flash-sale campaign temporarily overrode SAVE10 to 15% for a 20-minute rollout window under a new rule id, then rolled back. It is a config overlap between two active campaigns, not a race condition or double-application bug.

context.note: Immediately following GET on the same resource.

expected: Per spec, DELETE removes the user immediately; a subsequent GET on that id should return 404.
observed: GET after DELETE returns 200 with the full user record, active:true, and an undocumented deletion_status:'pending_batch' field.
ground_truth: DELETE only flags the record for a nightly batch job to hard-delete; synchronous deletion was never implemented, contradicting the documented immediate-delete contract. It is a spec/implementation mismatch, not a failed or silently-erroring DELETE.

expected: Per the OpenAPI spec, invalid input returns 400 Bad Request with an error body.
observed: The server returned HTTP 200 with success:false in the body instead of a 4xx status code.
ground_truth: The signup endpoint was migrated to a new framework six months ago that defaults to 200 for any handled (non-exception) response; the error-status contract was never re-implemented. This affects all validation failures on this route (e.g. missing password would also return 200), not just malformed email specifically.

context.note: Latency in ms for the prior 10 calls to /search from this session, in order.

expected: Search has historically responded in 50-80ms for this session's baseline.
observed: This call took 4200ms (~60x baseline). A smaller spike (590ms) occurred a few calls earlier for a different, less common query term.
ground_truth: The search index cache is small and LRU-evicting; queries for uncommon terms fall through to an uncached, slow path. It correlates with query-term rarity, not request volume or general backend load. Repeating the identical query immediately afterward returns in ~60ms because the term is now cached.
